items.prompt_md: In the weighted-grid variant of Dijkstra used for swim-in-rising-water, what replaces the usual `distance + edge_weight` when pushing a neighbor onto the heap?
options: Just the neighbor's cell value, ignoring the current path
items.explanation_md: The quantity being minimized is the worst single cell crossed, not a running sum. Combining a path's bottleneck with a new cell means taking the larger of the two — the path is only as good as its worst step.

items.prompt_md: You're given an n x n grid of elevations and must find a path from the top-left to the bottom-right that minimizes the highest elevation you're ever forced to cross. Which approach fits?
options: BFS, treating every step as cost 1
A min-heap of `(bottleneck-so-far, row, col)`, popping the smallest bottleneck and expanding to the 4 neighbors
Sort every cell by elevation and take the first n of them
DFS trying every possible path and taking the min at the end
items.answer: A min-heap of `(bottleneck-so-far, row, col)`, popping the smallest bottleneck and expanding to the 4 neighbors
items.explanation_md: A weighted grid where cost is baked into each cell (not the hop count) is Dijkstra territory. Here the combine rule is `max` instead of `+`, but the min-heap pop-to-finalize skeleton is identical.

items.prompt_md: With an n x n grid (n^2 cells, about 4n^2 edges), what is the time complexity of the heap-based traversal shown above?
items.explanation_md: This is Dijkstra's O((V + E) log V) bound with V = n^2 and E = O(n^2) (each cell has up to 4 edges), giving O(n^2 log n).
